item.title: Your AI-Ready Future
item.dc:creator: admin
item.excerpt:encoded: 5-minute starter on AI literacy, prompt engineering, and the skills students will need in an AI-shaped future.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Quick discussion on what it means to be truly AI-literate and how to write effective prompts.
a:2:{i:0;s:45:"Load slides 1–4 on the projector or board.";i:1;s:99:"Optional: have one simple example of a weak prompt and an improved prompt to show on screen.";}

a:2:{i:0;a:3:{s:4:"term";s:11:"AI Literacy";s:10:"definition";s:156:"The ability to understand, use, and critically evaluate AI technologies — including how they work, their limitations, and how to use them effectively and ethically.";s:18:"key_stage_adapted";b:0;}i:1;a:3:{s:4:"term";s:18:"Prompt Engineering";s:10:"definition";s:130:"The skill of writing clear, specific instructions for AI systems so they produce better, more useful outputs, using good context and examples.";s:18:"key_stage_adapted";b:0;}}

a:3:{i:0;a:2:{s:9:"objective";s:75:"Understand that AI literacy is about critical thinking, not just using AI tools";s:10:"assessable";b:1;}i:1;a:2:{s:9:"objective";s:68:"Recognise prompt engineering as a key future skill for working with AI";s:10:"assessable";b:1;}i:2;a:2:{s:9:"objective";s:90:"Identify at least one habit that can make their own AI use more effective and responsible";s:10:"assessable";b:1;}}

a:4:{i:0;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:1;s:6:"action";s:88:"Display Slide 1 (title) and introduce the idea of being "AI-ready" for future study and work.";s:8:"duration";s:0:"";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 1";s:13:"student_action";s:0:"";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:0:"";}i:1;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:2;s:6:"action";s:80:"Show Slide 2 (starter or debate question) and ask students to discuss in pairs.";s:8:"duration";s:10:"60 seconds";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 2";s:13:"student_action";s:15:"Pair discussion";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:75:"Encourage students to think about both opportunities and risks, not only one side.";}i:2;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:3;s:6:"action";s:110:"Display Slide 3 (Did You Know facts) about AI literacy and the importance of good prompts, and read them aloud.";s:8:"duration";s:0:"";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 3";s:13:"student_action";s:0:"";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:88:"If time allows, briefly show a weak prompt and an improved prompt to make the concept concrete.";}i:3;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:4;s:6:"action";s:90:"Show Slide 4 (reflection or answers) and summarise the key habits of an AI-literate student.";s:8:"duration";s:0:"";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 4";s:13:"student_action";s:0:"";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:75:"Invite a few students to share one habit they want to try next time they use AI.";}}
In 10 years' time, what AI-related skills do you think will matter most for your future — and how could you start building them now?

a:3:{i:0;s:100:"What does it really mean to be "good with AI" — is it about speed, creativity, critical thinking, or something else?";i:1;s:95:"How might AI literacy help you in subjects you study now, or in future jobs that do not even exist yet?";i:2;s:78:"What is one way that good prompts can make AI outputs fairer, safer, or more accurate?";}

a:9:{i:0;s:100:"Being AI-literate means understanding strengths and limits, checking sources, and not believing everything an AI says.";i:1;s:140:"Future jobs are likely to expect people to work alongside AI tools, so being able to design good prompts and review outputs critically will be valuable.";i:2;s:103:"Skills like communication, creativity, collaboration, and ethics will still matter — AI tools cannot replace good judgement or teamwork.";i:3;s:95:"Clear, specific prompts with good context help AI give answers that are more accurate and less biased.";i:4;s:120:"Testing and refining prompts teaches students to think carefully about what they are really asking and what a useful answer would look like.";i:5;s:88:"Sharing example prompts and explaining why they work can help classmates build better habits too.";i:6;s:120:"Students can practise writing prompts that include background, task, format, and audience (for example: "You are a science revision coach…").";i:7;s:103:"They can make it a habit to fact-check important AI outputs against trusted sources instead of copying them directly.";i:8;s:95:"They can reflect after using AI: What did it do well? Where did it struggle? What would I change next time?";}

This starter is designed to move students beyond seeing AI as a shortcut for homework and towards thinking about long-term skills. Emphasise that AI literacy is about critical thinking, ethics, and collaboration, not just technical knowledge. If students already use AI tools, invite short examples of when a good prompt made a big difference. Link the discussion to careers or subjects they care about so the ideas feel relevant rather than abstract.

a:3:{s:7:"support";s:115:"Provide sentence starters for prompts (e.g. "You are a helpful maths tutor…") and a simple checklist for what makes a good prompt.";s:6:"stretch";s:120:"Challenge students to design and compare two prompts for the same task, explaining which is stronger and why.";s:4:"send";s:120:"Offer visual examples of prompts and outputs, and allow students to respond verbally or with short bullet points instead of long paragraphs.";}
a:1:{i:0;a:2:{s:8:"activity";s:88:"Ask students to create a short "AI-ready habits" checklist for your class or school newsletter.";s:4:"type";s:11:"next_lesson";}}
